⌕
Problem-Based SOAP Note
Tap a section to expand. Each problem gets its own S/O/A/P block.
WHAT IS A PROBLEM-BASED NOTE?

Unlike a single-block SOAP, the problem-based format lists each active issue separately — each with its own mini-assessment and plan. This is how attendings think, how notes are graded, and how you avoid burying the chest pain under the UTI.

Structure: S once (shared) → O once (shared) → A/P per problem, numbered and prioritized by severity.

S — SUBJECTIVE
▸
Patient's story, overnight events, symptoms
WHAT GOES IN SUBJECTIVE

One shared S block at the top — you don't repeat it per problem.

ELEMENTS TO INCLUDE
OVERNIGHT EVENTS
New fevers, desats, rhythm changes, falls, new complaints, nursing concerns. If nothing: "No acute overnight events."
HOW THE PATIENT FEELS TODAY
Quote them directly when helpful: "Patient reports feeling better, pain now 3/10 vs 8/10 yesterday." Always note trajectory — better, worse, or same.
SYMPTOM REVIEW FOR ACTIVE PROBLEMS
Brief symptom check relevant to your problem list — not a full ROS. Chest pain, dyspnea, nausea, bowel/urine output, ambulation.
COMMON INTERN MISTAKE
Writing a full HPI here. The S in a daily progress note is brief — 3–5 lines. Save the full narrative for admissions and H&Ps.
EXAMPLE SUBJECTIVE
55M HD#3 for CAP / sepsis.
No acute overnight events per nursing. Afebrile overnight.
Patient reports improved dyspnea — "breathing is easier." Cough productive, less than yesterday. Appetite returning. No new chest pain or hemoptysis. No diarrhea.
O — OBJECTIVE
▸
Vitals, exam, labs, imaging — what you measured
WHAT GOES IN OBJECTIVE

Also one shared O block. Present it in a logical order: vitals → I/Os → exam → relevant labs → imaging. Don't bury critical values.

VITALS — INCLUDE ALL 24H
Report Tmax (not just current), HR range, BP range, SpO₂ (and O₂ requirement). Always contextualize: Tmax 38.4°C at 02:00, currently 37.1°C.
T: Tmax / current  ·  HR: range  ·  BP: range  ·  RR  ·  SpO₂ on [device/FiO₂]  ·  Weight
I/Os (INPUTS / OUTPUTS)
24h I/O and cumulative balance when relevant (sepsis, CHF, AKI, post-op). I: 1800mL · O: 1200mL · Net: +600mL · Cumulative: +2.4L
PHYSICAL EXAM — PROBLEM-FOCUSED
Lead with general, then systems relevant to your problems. You don't need to document every system every day — focus on what changed and what's pertinent.
Gen: Alert, NAD, sitting up in bed
HEENT: Sclera non-icteric, MM moist
CV: RRR, no murmurs, no JVD, no peripheral edema
Pulm: Decreased BS at R base, dullness to percussion, no wheeze
Abd: Soft, non-tender, non-distended, BS present
Ext: Warm, no calf tenderness, no edema
Neuro: A+Ox3, CN grossly intact
Skin: No new rashes, wound clean/dry/intact
LABS — TREND, DON'T JUST LIST
Report today's value with yesterday's in parentheses. This shows trajectory at a glance. Bold or flag critical values.
WBC 11.2 (↓ from 18.4) · Hgb 9.1 (stable) · Plt 210
Na 138 · K 3.8 · Cl 104 · HCO₃ 24 · BUN 22 · Cr 1.1 (↓ from 1.6)
Lactate 1.2 (↓ from 3.8) · Procalcitonin 1.4 (↓ from 14.2)
Blood cultures: NGTD × 48h
COMMON INTERN MISTAKE
Copy-pasting a wall of labs without interpretation. Your attendings skim — put the signal up front. Abnormals and trends > a full panel.
A/P — ASSESSMENT & PLAN (per problem)
▸
The intellectual core of your note — your thinking
HOW TO STRUCTURE EACH PROBLEM

Number your problems. Put the most acute/life-threatening issue first, not the admitting diagnosis if something more urgent is happening. Each problem block has: name → one-line assessment → plan bullets.

PROBLEM BLOCK ANATOMY
#1. Community-Acquired Pneumonia
— Clinically improving: Tmax trending down, WBC 11 from 18, CXR stable R lower lobe infiltrate. Day 3 of CTX/azithromycin.
  • Continue ceftriaxone 1g IV q24h + azithromycin 500mg PO q24h
  • Target 5-day course — plan PO step-down tomorrow if tolerating
  • Follow up sputum culture — no growth at 48h
  • Maintain O₂ sat >94%, wean supplemental O₂ as tolerated
  • Repeat CXR in 6 weeks to confirm resolution (outpatient)
#2. Sepsis — resolving
— Source = CAP. Lactate normalized (1.2), hemodynamically stable, no pressors. MAP >65 throughout.
  • No further fluid boluses — net positive 2.4L, monitor for fluid overload
  • Blood cultures NGTD — continue to monitor
  • Daily lactate if clinical concern recurs
#3. AKI — resolving
— Cr trending down (1.1 from 1.6 peak). Likely prerenal in setting of sepsis + poor PO. Improving with hydration.
  • Continue IVF at reduced rate — transition to PO intake
  • Trend Cr daily
  • Avoid nephrotoxins — hold NSAIDs, dose-adjust renally cleared meds
  • Reassess need for BMP tomorrow
HOW TO WRITE THE ONE-LINE ASSESSMENT
This is the hardest part for interns. It should answer: "What is going on with this problem and why?" It should NOT just restate what you'll do.
✗ WEAK (no thinking)
CAP — will continue antibiotics.
✓ STRONG (shows synthesis)
CAP — improving clinically and biochemically (WBC ↓, Tmax ↓, lactate normalized). Likely typical bacterial pathogen (rapid antigen neg for flu, minimal atypical risk). On track for 5-day course.
PROBLEM PRIORITIZATION — HOW TO ORDER
1. Most life-threatening active problem first (sepsis, hemodynamic instability, ACS, airway)
2. Admitting diagnosis (if not already #1)
3. Active medical issues (AKI, electrolyte derangements, anemia)
4. Chronic disease management (DM, HTN, COPD — relevant to hospitalization)
5. Prophylaxis & disposition (DVT ppx, foley removal, diet, PT/OT, discharge planning)
STANDING PROBLEMS — ALWAYS ADDRESS
Even if "unchanged," briefly note status so readers know you checked:
#N. DVT prophylaxis — Enoxaparin 40mg SQ qd. No contraindications.
#N. Diet / nutrition — Advanced to regular diet, tolerating POs.
#N. Lines / catheters — PIV only. No Foley. Remove PIV if d/c tomorrow.
#N. Disposition — Target discharge HD#4 if continued improvement. PCP follow-up arranged.
COMMON INTERN MISTAKES
• Listing plans without assessments — every problem needs a one-line "why" before the bullets
• Forgetting discharge planning — start from day 1; attendings will ask every round
• Not addressing chronic meds — held home meds must appear and have a reason
• Missing the safety net — add what you'd watch for: "if worsening X → escalate to Y"
DAILY WORKFLOW — MORNING TO NOTE
▸
How to pre-round, present, and get your notes done
PRE-ROUND SEQUENCE
①
Pull overnight vitals & I/Os
Tmax, HR/BP ranges, O₂ req, fluid balance. Flag anything that changed overnight before you walk in the room.
②
Review morning labs
BMP, CBC, any cultures back, any new results. Note trends vs yesterday — that's the story.
③
Read nursing notes
Nurses document what they see hourly. Overnight events, behavior changes, patient complaints — often the most useful S data.
④
See the patient — exam & brief interview
"How did you sleep? Any new symptoms overnight? Pain now vs yesterday?" Then your exam. Takes 5–8 min. Don't skip it for notes.
⑤
Build your problem list & formulate plans
Before writing a word: decide your problems in priority order and your one-sentence assessment for each. Know your plan before you type it.
⑥
Write the note
S → O → A/P per problem. Target: note done before rounds so your attending can read it. A note on rounds is better than no note, but barely.
PRESENTATION FORMAT (1-LINER THEN SYSTEMS)
Opening sentence: [Name], [age][sex], HD#[X] for [primary diagnosis], now presenting with [major overnight development or "no acute overnight events"].
"Mr. Smith is a 55-year-old man, hospital day 3 for community-acquired pneumonia with sepsis, who has no acute overnight events and reports improvement in dyspnea."
Then: vitals → I/Os → exam findings → labs → plan per problem. Keep it to 3–4 minutes per patient or you will lose your team.
FULL EXAMPLE NOTE — CAP / SEPSIS / AKI
▸
Complete annotated progress note you can model
INTERNAL MEDICINE PROGRESS NOTE — HD#3
SUBJECTIVE
55M HD#3 for CAP with sepsis and AKI.
No acute overnight events per nursing. Afebrile since 0200.
Patient reports significant improvement in breathing — "I feel much better." Cough productive with decreased frequency. Tolerating oral fluids. No new chest pain, hemoptysis, or diarrhea. Ambulated to bathroom independently this morning.

OBJECTIVE
Vitals (24h): Tmax 38.4°C (0200) → 37.1°C now | HR 78–102 | BP 112–134/70–84 | RR 18 | SpO₂ 95% on 2L NC (was 4L) | Wt 82kg
I/O (24h): In 2100mL / Out 1750mL / Net +350mL / Cumulative +2.8L

Exam:
Gen: Alert, NAD, conversational, sitting up eating breakfast
HEENT: MM moist, no LAD
CV: RRR, no murmurs/rubs/gallops, no JVD
Pulm: Dullness to percussion at R base, decreased BS R>L, no wheeze, improved from yesterday
Abd: Soft, non-tender, non-distended, normoactive BS
Ext: Warm, well-perfused, trace bilateral ankle edema (new — likely resuscitation-related), no calf tenderness
Neuro: A+Ox3, appropriate

Labs:
WBC 11.2 (↓18.4) | Hgb 9.8 (stable) | Plt 210
Na 138 | K 4.1 | Cr 1.1 (↓1.6 peak) | BUN 24 | HCO₃ 23
Lactate 1.2 (↓3.8) | Procalcitonin 1.4 (↓14.2)
BCx ×2: NGTD at 60h | Sputum: GPC in pairs, moderate PMNs, final pending

Imaging: CXR (AM): R lower lobe consolidation, stable vs yesterday. No new effusion.

ASSESSMENT & PLAN
#1. Community-Acquired Pneumonia — Clinically improving
Tmax trending down, WBC halved, O₂ requirement reduced from 4L→2L NC. Sputum Gram stain with GPC — S. pneumoniae most likely. On track for 5-day course.
  • Continue ceftriaxone 1g IV q24h; plan PO amoxicillin 1g TID step-down tomorrow if tolerating
  • Continue azithromycin 500mg PO q24h (day 3 of 5)
  • Titrate O₂ to SpO₂ >94%; wean to RA as tolerated
  • Await final sputum cultures — narrow if sensitivities available
  • Repeat CXR at discharge; outpatient follow-up CXR in 6 weeks

#2. Sepsis — Resolved
Source = CAP. Lactate normal, hemodynamically stable ×24h, no vasopressors. Fluid resuscitation complete.
  • Antibiotics as above
  • Hold further IVF — cumulative positive 2.8L, note trace edema
  • Blood cultures NGTD — no source control issue

#3. AKI (Cr 1.6 → 1.1) — Resolving
Prerenal etiology in setting of sepsis-related hypoperfusion and poor PO intake. Responding to IV hydration. No contrast, no nephrotoxin exposure.
  • Advance diet — encourage oral fluids; d/c maintenance IVF
  • Trend Cr daily; target BMP tomorrow
  • Hold NSAIDs indefinitely; renally dose any new meds
  • If Cr not trending to baseline by discharge → outpatient nephrology referral

#4. New trace bilateral ankle edema
Likely resuscitation-related fluid shift rather than cardiac decompensation — no JVD, no new dyspnea, HR controlled. Low suspicion for new CHF in this context.
  • No diuresis at this time — will reassess in 24h
  • If respiratory status worsens or edema increases → repeat BNP, consider echo

#5. DM Type 2 (home: metformin)
Metformin held on admission (AKI + contrast risk). Fingersticks 120–180 range on hospital insulin protocol.
  • Continue sliding scale insulin
  • Resume metformin at discharge once Cr at baseline

#6. Prophylaxis / Safety
  • DVT ppx: Enoxaparin 40mg SQ qd — no contraindications
  • GI ppx: Not indicated (no high-risk features, not on steroids)
  • Lines: PIV only. No Foley. No central line.
  • Diet: Regular diet; advance as tolerated
  • Activity: Ambulate TID with nursing

#7. Disposition
Target discharge HD#4 pending: continued clinical improvement, Cr trending to baseline, O₂ wean to RA, tolerating PO antibiotics. PCP follow-up within 1 week. Discharge CXR planned.
PEARLS & PITFALLS
▸
What separates a good intern note from a bad one
✦ THINK IN PROBLEMS, NOT SYSTEMS
A system-based note (CV, Pulm, Renal…) forces the reader to mentally regroup. A problem-based note mirrors how consultants and attendings think — and it makes your reasoning visible.
✦ YOUR ASSESSMENT IS YOUR MOST IMPORTANT SENTENCE
Anyone can copy-paste labs. The one-line synthesis ("improving/worsening/stable because X") is what demonstrates you understand the patient. This is what attendings evaluate.
✦ TREND EVERYTHING
Cr 1.1 means nothing. Cr 1.1 (↓ from 1.6 yesterday, peak 2.2 on admission) tells the whole story. Always show direction and time context.
✦ HELD HOME MEDS MUST APPEAR
Every held home medication needs to show up on your problem list — with a reason (AKI, NPO, renal dosing) and a plan to restart. Missing this is a patient safety issue and a billing issue.
✦ DISCHARGE PLANNING STARTS ON DAY 1
What criteria need to be met for discharge? What follow-up does the patient need? Who needs to be notified (PCP, subspecialty)? Write this on every note — even day 1. It forces you to define your endpoint.
✦ ADDRESS THE INCIDENTAL FINDINGS
Lung nodule on CT done for another reason. Mildly elevated ALT. Incidentaloma. These must appear in your note — even if just "incidentally noted, will follow up outpatient." Ignoring them creates medicolegal exposure.
✦ NEVER SIGN A NOTE YOU HAVEN'T ACTED ON
If you write "check BMP tomorrow" — order it. If you write "discuss with cardiology" — call them. Your note is a contract. Your attending will verify your actions match your note on rounds.
✦ THE "IF/THEN" RULE
For any unstable problem, include what you're watching for and what you'll do: "If respiratory status worsens or O₂ req increases → CXR, consider HFNC, pulm consult." This shows anticipatory thinking — attendings love it.
Medical language
Pick a system → the precise, codeable diagnosis name, the numbers that define it, and a sample A&P sentence you can copy.
⇄ CONNECTOR SHORTHAND — read me first
▸
These are the glue words that turn a list of findings into a real assessment. Master these and your one-liner reads like a senior's.
TERM MEANING EXAMPLE
2/2Secondary to / due to"Hypoxic resp failure 2/2 PNA"
s/pStatus post"s/p intubation"
r/oRule out"R/o PE"
c/wConsistent with"Labs c/w DKA"
vsVersus (differential)"PNA vs aspiration vs HAP"
in setting ofContext / backdrop"Hyperglycemia in setting of sepsis"
likelyMost probable"Likely UTI source"
possiblyLess certain"Possibly aspiration"
complicatingResulting from"AKI complicating sepsis"
perAccording to"Per attending..."
▤ THE ONE-LINER FORMULA — read me first
▸
MODEL EVERY ASSESSMENT ON THIS
"[Age/gender] with [CDI-specific condition] 2/2 [etiology], c/w [supporting finding]. Likely [most probable] vs [alternative] vs [cannot rule out]."
⚑ ABOUT SPECIFICITY — don't overdo it
Use the most specific codeable diagnosis when one exists — capture acuity + chronicity + severity (this drives Severity-of-Illness / Risk-of-Mortality). "Acute hypoxic respiratory failure," not "hypoxia." "Acute on chronic systolic HF exacerbation," not "CHF." But not every diagnosis has a special "specific version" — many are just stated plainly (e.g. "acute appendicitis," "cellulitis"). Don't force specificity where it doesn't apply or invent severity you can't support.
⌕
Assessment & plan
The fellow-level framework — for your written A&P and how you present it out loud on rounds. Goal: make your reasoning visible so you sound like a fellow, not a day-3 intern. Tap a section to expand.
THE CORE IDEA

An intern note answers: what is going on, and what are we doing?

A fellow note answers: what is going on, what is the central tension, why this path over the alternatives, and what am I worried about next?

The entire gap between them is clinical reasoning made visible. The four layers below close that gap.

Presenting on rounds is the same reasoning out loud — and it's judged in real time, in your first sentence. Everything here works for both the note and the verbal presentation.

★ START HERE — the fastest path (day 3 → fellow)
▸

You can't fix everything at once. Here's the order that moves the needle fastest — master each before the next.

1. Nail the one-liner. If you fix nothing else, fix this. It frames every presentation and is what seniors judge in the first ten seconds. (See the next section.)
2. Lead with your assessment, not your data. Say what you think, then the why. Don't read labs out loud hoping a diagnosis appears.
3. Commit + anticipate. Pick a working diagnosis ("...until proven otherwise") and say what you're watching for next.
THE DAILY PRACTICE LOOP (deliberate reps)
① Pick ONE patient. Write the one-liner cold, then rebuild it with the formula. (5 min)
② Say the full A&P out loud before rounds — to a co-intern, the mirror, or a phone voice memo. Listen back once.
③ Pre-game the pimp. Write the 1–2 questions you'll get and your answers.
④ Steal one move. After rounds, grab ONE phrase or move the fellow used that you didn't. Keep a running "steal list."
⑤ Weekly, fold your steal list into your default.
Reps + feedback + stealing from seniors. That's the whole fast path. Two weeks of this and you'll hear the difference.
◎ THE ONE-LINER — your highest-yield skill
▸

Your opening summary statement is the single highest-yield thing you say. Seniors form their read of you in this one sentence. In med-ed it's your problem representation — and nailing it is the clearest marker of expertise.

THE FORMULA
[age/sex] with [problem-relevant PMH + qualifiers] presenting with [time course + syndrome] now [trajectory / current status]
THE FOUR RULES
1. Curate the PMH. Only the 2–3 comorbidities that bear on today. Drop the 1995 appendectomy when the problem is chest pain — relevance is expertise.
2. Lead with the syndrome, not the verbatim complaint. "Hypoxemic respiratory failure," not "shortness of breath." Abstract upward.
3. Pack in semantic qualifiers. acute/chronic, exertional/rest, first-episode/recurrent. (See "The language of a fellow.")
4. End with the trajectory, not a snapshot — "improving on day 2," "now off pressors," "worsening despite diuresis."
WEAK → STRONG
"68-year-old man with diabetes, hypertension, high cholesterol, GERD, and prior knee surgery here with chest pain and some shortness of breath."
"68M with HFrEF (EF 30%), multivessel CAD, and CKD3 presenting with 2 days of exertional dyspnea and orthopnea, now improving after 3 L diuresis on hospital day 2."
A crisp one-liner declares your lens — and the rest of the presentation almost writes itself. Use the LANGUAGE tab for the codeable syndrome name that belongs in the middle slot.
▦ THE 4-LAYER ANATOMY OF A STRONG A&P
▸
# LAYER WHAT IT DOES WITHOUT IT...
1ImpressionStaged problem list, data inlineReads like a billing list
2Narrative3–5 sentence reasoning paragraphThe note has no brain
3PlanNumbered problems, lettered orders, logicOrders look arbitrary
4AnticipationWhat you're worried about next, and whyA snapshot, not a trajectory
LAYER 1 — THE IMPRESSION
▸

Each problem line should carry its own evidence and classification — the reader learns the severity and the data without scrolling down.

THE FORMULA
# [Diagnosis] ([key data inline]) - [stage/class] - [data gap or status]
WEAK → STRONG
# HFrEF
→ # Ischemic cardiomyopathy (LVEF 50-55% → 25-30%, 6/23) - ACC Stage C, NYHA III
# NSTEMI
→ # NSTEMI (trop peak 19k, 6/23)
# Hx lung CA
→ # Hx SCLC w/ mediastinal mets s/p XRT (2021) - in remission per chart review
THE THREE HABITS
1. Put the key number inline. Trop peak, EF trajectory, P/F, Cr, the date it changed — diagnosis and evidence travel together.
2. Stage or classify whenever a system exists. This single move signals sophistication.
3. Name your data gaps honestly. "No prior PFT," "per chart review," "pending" — shows you verified rather than assumed.
STAGING SYSTEMS TO MEMORIZE
Heart failureNYHA I–IV + ACC/AHA Stage A–D
COPDGOLD 1–4 + GOLD A/B/E
CKD / AKIKDIGO stage / G & A categories
LiverChild-Pugh A/B/C, MELD
SepsisSIRS / qSOFA / Sepsis-3
ARDSBerlin (mild/mod/severe by P/F)
PneumoniaCURB-65 / PSI
PancreatitisRanson / BISAP
GI bleedGlasgow-Blatchford / Rockall
Rule: if a staging system exists, use it. An unstaged diagnosis is the most common intern tell. (See the CRITERIA tab for the actual cutoffs.)
LAYER 2 — THE NARRATIVE PARAGRAPH
▸

The single biggest upgrade you can make — the difference between a note with a brain and one without. 3–5 sentences, placed between the impression and the plan. Write one move per sentence, in order:

1 · STATE THE CENTRAL TENSION
Open with the competing-priorities axis that governs the case. "She sits between needing anticoagulation for unprotected left main disease and actively bleeding from her gut."
2 · REASON THE DECISION + RULE OUT ALTERNATIVES
Say why the leading option wins and why the roads not taken are off the table. "Not a surgical candidate given active melena, and PCI is prohibitively high-risk while she bleeds due to the heparin required."
3 · ADDRESS UNCERTAINTY, THEN COMMIT
Don't fence-sit. Acknowledge the ambiguity, then commit to the more dangerous dx. "Despite features of stress cardiomyopathy, the angiogram matches the wall-motion territory, so this is coronary ischemia until proven otherwise."
4 · ANTICIPATE THE NEXT PROBLEM + MECHANISM
Predict what's coming and name the mechanism. "Very high-risk for recurrent flash pulmonary edema given significant MR and ongoing ischemia — watch BPs closely as we transition diuretics."
✦ THE "UNTIL PROVEN OTHERWISE" MOVE
When two diagnoses compete: commit to the more dangerous one as the working dx, state which data supports it, and use the literal phrase "...is [dangerous dx] until proven otherwise." It shows you refuse to be falsely reassured — one of the highest-yield phrases you can adopt.
LAYER 3 — THE PLAN
▸

Numbered problems, lettered orders. Each sub-item a discrete, executable order — not a sentence. Build conditional logic and a trajectory into every problem.

1 · DOSE, ROUTE, FREQUENCY — EVERY TIME
"diurese" → "Bumex 1 mg IV → bumex gtt @ 0.5 mg/hr"
2 · BUILD IN CONDITIONAL / CONTINGENCY LOGIC
"if X then Y" shows a trajectory, not a snapshot. "Metop IV 2.5 mg and if tolerates → tartrate 25 mg 1–2x/day."
3 · A HOLD NEEDS A RESTART TRIGGER
"Hold ARB/SGLT2i/MRA for now but layer on once more stable." · "Hold metoprolol, restart when euvolemic and hemodynamically stable."
4 · GIVE TARGETS, NOT JUST ACTIONS
"Nitro gtt — wean as able, goal SBP >100 and <130." · "Replete lytes for goal K >4, Mg >2."
LAYER 4 — ANTICIPATION & CROSS-PROBLEM AWARENESS
▸

A snapshot note documents what happened; a trajectory note predicts what's coming. This is what gets you trusted overnight.

A · FORWARD-LOOKING ANTICIPATION
For each major problem add: what I'm worried about next + the mechanism. "Very high risk for recurrent pulmonary edema (significant MR, ischemia ± ischemic MR) — will watch BPs closely."
B · CROSS-PROBLEM WEAVING
Connect systems where they interact. "Higher-risk for endoscopy given mild volume overload, hypoxia from PNA/COPD, and significant CAD — agree with GI plan to intubate if proceeding." The GI plan references the cardiac AND pulmonary problems.
🔊 SAYING IT OUT LOUD — delivery on rounds
▸

The note is read; the presentation is performed. Same reasoning, different delivery. How to make it land:

LEAD WITH THE BOTTOM LINE
One-liner → "X active problems" → then go problem by problem. Don't make them sit through a data dump waiting for your impression.
SYNTHESIZE, DON'T READ
Per problem: name it, give your one-sentence assessment (the why), then the plan. Glance at the screen for a number — never read labs in a list.
SIGNPOST OUT LOUD
"Three active problems. First, the heart failure... Second, the GI bleed..." Audible numbering tells the listener exactly where you are.
PACE + PAUSE
Deliver the one-liner, then pause one beat — let it land. Slow down for the assessment; speed through stable, unchanged problems.
TIME DISCIPLINE
A stable floor patient is ~2 minutes. The part you never rush is the assessment + plan. Compression is a fellow skill — if it's stable and unchanged, say so and move on.
⌥ THE LANGUAGE OF A FELLOW — word choice
▸
A · SEMANTIC QUALIFIERS (the words experts use)
Paired opposites that sharpen a problem: acute ↔ chronic ↔ subacute · sudden ↔ gradual · exertional ↔ at rest · pleuritic ↔ positional ↔ pressure · productive ↔ dry · first-episode ↔ recurrent · localized ↔ diffuse · progressive ↔ stable. Two qualifiers per problem instantly raises your register.
B · PERTINENT NEGATIVES (reason in two directions)
State what you didn't find that changes the differential. "Afebrile, no leukocytosis, lactate 1.0 — arguing against sepsis as the driver." Positives support; negatives refute. Fellows always give both.
C · CALIBRATED CONFIDENCE (delete the hedges)
Cut "I think maybe it could possibly be." Use the confidence ladder and match the word to your certainty:
no evidence for → argues against → unlikely → can't exclude → favor → most consistent with → diagnostic of.
State pretest probability when you can ("high pretest probability for PE") — it justifies the test.
🔗 THE CONNECTOR GRAMMAR — 2/2 vs c/w vs iso vs vs (NOT synonyms)
▸

These tiny phrases sound interchangeable but each makes a different logical claim. Picking the right one is what makes your reasoning legible. The trick: ask what is this word actually claiming?

A problem statement isn't a list — it's one argument: [name the problem, staged] → [how sure: likely/possibly] → [the CAUSE: 2/2 / iso] → [the EVIDENCE: c/w + data] → [the alternative: vs] → trajectory.

2/2 · secondary to  CLAIMS: CAUSE
"X is caused by Y." Use it when you name the etiology and you're committing. Direction: effect 2/2 cause.
"Hypoxic respiratory failure 2/2 multifocal PNA."
c/w · consistent with  CLAIMS: EVIDENCE
"The data fit diagnosis X." It justifies a dx from findings — it does NOT name a cause. Drop it right after you cite data.
"Anion-gap acidosis with ketones, c/w DKA."
iso · in the setting of  CLAIMS: CONTEXT
"X is happening against the backdrop of Y — related, contributing, but I'm not committing to causation." Use when there are several contributors.
"Hyperglycemia iso sepsis and steroids."
complicating  CLAIMS: DOWNSTREAM COMPLICATION
Same causal link as 2/2 but framed from the primary problem's side: "new problem X arose from existing problem Y." Flags it as a sequela that worsens the course.
"AKI complicating sepsis"  =  "AKI 2/2 sepsis," but emphasizes it as a hit.
vs · versus  CLAIMS: DIFFERENTIAL
"X or Y, ranked, not yet resolved." The competing etiologies you're still working through. Pairs with likely.
"Likely prerenal AKI, vs ATN." · "PNA vs aspiration vs HAP."
likely / possibly  CLAIMS: CERTAINTY LEVEL
Not a relationship — your confidence. likely = leading, you're acting on it; possibly = on the list, lower. They tune 2/2 / c/w, they don't replace them.
"AKI, likely 2/2 prerenal, possibly early ATN."
r/o · rule out  CLAIMS: A TASK
A can't-miss to actively exclude — an action item, not a probability. "r/o" something dangerous even if unlikely.
"Chest pain — r/o ACS, PE, dissection."
s/p status post  ·  per according to  CLAIMS: TIMELINE / ATTRIBUTION
s/p = a completed event, no claim ("s/p PCI 2021"). per = attributing a statement to a source ("per cardiology, no cath today").
✦ ONE SENTENCE, EVERY CONNECTOR DOING ITS JOB
"AKI complicating sepsis, likely 2/2 prerenal physiology iso poor PO intake — FENa <1 and bland sediment c/w a prerenal picture — vs ATN; will r/o obstruction with renal US, s/p 2 L LR per renal."
complicating = came from the sepsis · likely = confidence · 2/2 = the cause · iso = the contributing context · c/w = the evidence · vs = the live alternative · r/o = the can't-miss task · s/p = done · per = whose call.
See the worked cases below to watch these slot into real DKA / withdrawal / sepsis problem statements.
⏱ WHEN & HOW OFTEN — do I use these every time? (no)
▸

No — you do not sprinkle these on every line. Each connector is just the word for a reasoning move. You use it only when you're actually making that move.

Stop asking "which connectors should I add?" Ask "what am I claiming right now?" Connectors follow your thinking — they don't generate it. A clean, confident line with zero connectors beats a connector salad.

ASK YOURSELF (pick only what applies)
Naming a cause I'm confident in? → 2/2 (+ likely if hedging)
Showing the data that support a diagnosis? → c/w
Two+ causes genuinely still in play? → vs (put likely on the leader)
A contributing backdrop I won't pin as THE cause? → iso
One problem caused another? → complicating
A dangerous dx to actively exclude? → r/o
Completed event? → s/p  ·  Whose rec? → per
None apply? Say it plainly — no connector needed.
HOW OFTEN — BY LOCATION
One-liner — usually one move: a single c/w or 2/2, occasionally a vs. Often zero when the dx is established.
"68M with HFrEF admitted with an acute exacerbation, now improving after diuresis." — needs none.
Narrative / per-problem assessment — where they cluster, because you're showing the argument: 2/2, c/w, vs, likely, r/o.
Plan — mostly r/o (the can't-miss to exclude), plus holds & targets.
MATCH THE WORD TO YOUR CERTAINTY
Confirmed (diagnostic ECG, positive culture) → just name it. "STEMI," not "likely STEMI."
Leading but unconfirmed → likely 2/2.
Genuinely split → vs.   Dangerous but unlikely → r/o.
✗ THE TELLS (don't do these)
• Connector salad — stuffing all of them in one sentence to sound smart. It sounds forced.
• A fake vs when you're actually sure (padding the differential).
• c/w then restating you're certain — pick one.
• 2/2 a cause you couldn't defend if pimped "why?" — commit only to what you can justify.
• r/o-ing a reflexive laundry list instead of the specific killers for THIS presentation.
SAME PATIENT, THREE REGISTERS
UNDERCOOKED (no reasoning)
"Patient with kidney problems and low blood pressure."
RIGHT (each word doing a job)
"AKI, likely 2/2 prerenal from GI losses, vs ATN — FENa <1 c/w prerenal; will r/o obstruction with US."
OVERDONE (salad — forced & unreadable)
"AKI 2/2 vs c/w prerenal iso possibly likely ATN complicating everything, r/o all causes..."
Bottom line: name the move you're making, use that one word, move on. The number of connectors should equal the number of reasoning moves — no more.
⚔ DEFEND YOUR REASONING — the pimp & the challenge
▸
PRE-LOAD THE LIKELY QUESTION
Before you present, ask "what will they push on?" — usually "why not [the alternative]?" Put that answer in your narrative and they can't catch you.
THE "WHY NOT X" REFLEX
For your leading dx, always carry the top alternative and the one reason it's lower. "Most consistent with NSTEMI; I considered PE but no hypoxia, no tachycardia, low pretest probability."
WHEN YOU DON'T KNOW, SAY SO — WITH A PLAN
"I don't know, but here's how I'd find out." Never bluff — it's the fastest way to lose trust. A confident "I don't know + next step" sounds more senior than a wrong guess.
NAME YOUR CAN'T-MISS
"My biggest concern is X; if I'm wrong, the thing I can't afford to miss is Y, so I've covered for it with Z." This is exactly how fellows think out loud.
🗂 THE FELLOW PHRASE BANK — steal these
▸

The actual phrases that signal seniority. Borrow them until they're yours. Grouped by what they do:

FRAME / SYNTHESIZE
"The unifying diagnosis is..." · "The central issue is..." · "Putting it together, this is..." · "The story that fits everything is..."
COMMIT
"Most consistent with..." · "This is X until proven otherwise." · "I'm treating this as X." · "My working diagnosis is..."
PRIORITIZE
"The problem driving the admission is..." · "The can't-miss here is..." · "Top of my differential is..."
HEDGE APPROPRIATELY
"I can't fully exclude X, so I've covered for it with..." · "Lower on my differential, but worth a rule-out..." · "Pending [test], but I'm not waiting to treat."
ANTICIPATE
"What I'm watching for is..." · "If X happens, the plan is Y." · "The next 24 hours will tell us..." · "I'm worried about [complication] via [mechanism]."
CONNECT PROBLEMS
"These are likely the same process." · "Her kidneys and her heart are competing — diuresing one stresses the other." · "This is all downstream of..."
LEAN ON THE EVIDENCE
"Per [guideline/trial]..." · "GDMT-wise, she's on..." · "The data support..." (see the TRIALS tab)
HONEST UNKNOWN
"I don't have that yet — I've sent for it." · "That's a gap; I'll read on it tonight." · "I'd ask [consultant] to weigh in on..."
✂ COMMON INTERN TELLS — delete these
▸

The specific habits that instantly mark you as junior. Cut these and you climb a level before you add anything. Stop saying the ✗, start saying the ✓.

✗ Reading labs one by one — "sodium 138, potassium 4.0, chloride 104..."
✓ Synthesize: "Labs notable for an AKI, Cr up to 2.1, with a gap of 18." Quote only what drives a decision.
✗ "Patient is a poor historian."
✓ "History limited by encephalopathy — obtained collateral from family and chart." Name the reason, say what you did about it.
✗ Burying the lede — opening with social history or a 1995 surgery.
✓ Lead with the active syndrome in your one-liner; bring in history only where it's relevant.
✗ Over-hedging — "I think it might possibly maybe be..."
✓ Commit with calibrated confidence: "Most consistent with X."
✗ The unstaged diagnosis — "CHF," "AKI," "cirrhosis."
✓ Stage everything with a system: "acute on chronic HFrEF, NYHA III" · "AKI, KDIGO 2."
✗ Data → orders with no assessment in between.
✓ Say what you think first — one assessment sentence per problem, then the plan.
✗ "Will continue current management / will monitor."
✓ Monitor for WHAT, with what trigger: "watching UOP; if <0.5 mL/kg/h over 6h, fluid challenge."
✗ "Patient is stable" (when they're not) / quoting only the current vital.
✓ Give the trend: "Tmax 38.9 overnight, now 37.2; HR down from 110s to 80s."
✗ The kitchen-sink differential — 12 items, no ranking.
✓ Prioritize: most likely + the can't-miss. "Most likely X; can't-miss is Y, covered by Z."
✗ "Noncompliant patient."
✓ "Nonadherent — barrier is cost / access / understanding." Neutral, and it points to a fix.
✗ A number with no trend — "BNP 1850."
✓ Anchor it: "BNP 1850, up from 400 at admission."
✗ Filler & apology — "um, so, sorry, I think..."
✓ A confident pause beats filler. State it, then stop talking.
✗ "Do you want me to...?" for routine decisions.
✓ Propose: "I'd like to start X — unless you'd prefer otherwise." Lead; don't outsource the thinking.
◆ SUBSPECIALTY LENSES — sound like a fellow on THAT service
▸

Same patient, same A&P — but each service sees it through its own lens and leads with what it cares about. Adopt that lens (and its anchors and phrases) and you sound like one of them. On a consult, frame your one-liner around the consulting service's lens.

CARDIOLOGY
Lens: tell the hemodynamic story — pump, pipes, rhythm, or volume? Forward (perfusion) vs backward (congestion) failure.
Anchors on: EF + trajectory · ischemic territory (which vessel/wall) · the warm/cold–wet/dry profile · rhythm · the 4 GDMT pillars · troponin/BNP trend · preload/afterload/contractility.
Say: "She's warm and wet — congested but perfusing." · "This is a supply–demand question." · "GDMT-wise she's on a beta-blocker and ARNI, not yet on an SGLT2i or MRA." · "The wall-motion abnormality matches the LAD territory." · "Which lever do we pull — preload, afterload, or contractility?"
Flavor one-liner: "68M with ischemic cardiomyopathy (EF 25–30%) presenting warm-and-wet with an acute HFrEF exacerbation 2/2 dietary nonadherence, now diuresing on a bumetanide drip — will optimize GDMT once euvolemic."
PULMONARY / CRITICAL CARE
Lens: gas exchange + mechanics + the shock state. Oxygenation (A-a gradient, P/F) vs ventilation (CO₂). Classify the shock.
Anchors on: P/F ratio · A-a gradient · vent settings (Vt/kg IBW, PEEP, plateau & driving pressure, compliance) · ABG trend · shock type (distributive/cardiogenic/hypovolemic/obstructive) · lactate clearance · fluid-responsiveness.
Say: "Hypoxemic vs hypercapnic failure." · "Widened A-a gradient — so V/Q mismatch or shunt, not hypoventilation." · "Lung-protective, driving pressure under 15." · "Distributive shock until proven otherwise." · "Is she fluid-responsive?"
NEPHROLOGY
Lens: three questions — what's the volume status, what's the gap/acid-base, what does the urine say? Then localize pre/intra/post.
Anchors on: Cr vs the patient's baseline · UOP · FENa/FEUrea · urine sediment (casts) · urine lytes · anion gap + compensation · K and acid-base · the volume exam.
Say: "Pre, intra, or post?" · "FENa under 1 — prerenal physiology." · "Muddy-brown casts, so this is ATN." · "Gap of 24 with appropriate compensation by Winter's." · "The urine tells the story."
INFECTIOUS DISEASE
Lens: source → bug → drug → duration, plus source control. Always: "what's the source, and is it controlled?"
Anchors on: suspected source · gram stain + culture data · the local antibiogram/susceptibilities · spectrum (de-escalate!) · source-control status · the duration clock · immune status.
Say: "What's the source, and is it controlled?" · "I'd de-escalate to [narrow agent] per susceptibilities." · "Day 3 of a 7-day course." · "This needs source control, not just antibiotics."
GI / HEPATOLOGY
Lens: separate the lesion from the function. In liver: synthetic function + portal-HTN physiology.
Anchors on: hemodynamic stability + transfusion · bleeding stigmata (Forrest) · Child-Pugh / MELD · INR + albumin (synthetic function) · encephalopathy grade · the SAAG.
Say: "Variceal or non-variceal?" · "Child-Pugh B, MELD 18." · "Decompensated cirrhosis — name which decompensation." · "Her synthetic function is failing."
HEME / ONC
Lens: mechanism first. Anemia & thrombocytopenia: production vs destruction vs sequestration/loss. Look at the smear.
Anchors on: the smear · retic count · cell line(s) involved · MCV · hemolysis labs · the mechanism · stage + performance status (ECOG).
Say: "Production, destruction, or sequestration?" · "What does the smear show?" · "Retic-appropriate or not?" · "Her ECOG is 1."
ENDOCRINE
Lens: it's an axis — think feedback loops. Confirm with the dynamic test. Treat the patient, not the number.
Anchors on: the hormone + its trophic partner (TSH/free T4, ACTH/cortisol, PTH/Ca) · the dynamic / stim / suppression test · the precipitant.
Say: "Primary vs secondary — check the trophic hormone." · "Confirm with a stim test." · "Treat the patient, not the TSH."
NEUROLOGY
Lens: localize first (where is the lesion?), then etiology. The exam IS the test. Often time-critical.
Anchors on: the localizing exam · time-last-known-well · NIHSS · the vascular territory · the imaging.
Say: "Localize first: cortex, brainstem, cord, or nerve?" · "Time is brain — last known well?" · "The exam localizes to..."
▤ THE FULL SKELETON — copy this
▸
IMPRESSION:
# [Dx] ([key data inline]) - [stage/class]
# [Dx] ([key data]) - [data gap / status]
# [Dx] ...

NARRATIVE (3–5 sentences):
1. Open with the central clinical tension.
2. Reason the major decision + why alternatives are off the table.
3. Address uncertainty → commit ("...until proven otherwise").
4. Anticipate the next likely problem + the mechanism.

RECOMMENDATIONS:
1. [Problem]
   a. [discrete order — drug/dose/route/freq]
   b. [conditional logic — "if tolerates, then..."]
   c. [hold + restart trigger]
   d. [target/goal]
2. [Problem]
   a. ...
✓ WORKED EXAMPLE (annotated)
▸
SPOKEN ONE-LINER — how you'd open on rounds
"Older woman with multivessel CAD (90% distal left main), ischemic cardiomyopathy with EF down to 25–30%, and moderate–severe MR, admitted with an NSTEMI now complicated by a new GI bleed — the central tension is she needs anticoagulation for her coronaries but is actively bleeding."
IMPRESSION — data inline + staging + gaps
# NSTEMI (19k peak 6/23)
# CAD: 90% distal LM/ostial LAD + CTO of RCA
# Ischemic cardiomyopathy (LVEF 50-55% → 25-30%, 6/23) - ACC Stage C, NYHA III
# Moderate–severe MR
# Emphysema - no prior PFT
# Hx SCLC w/ mediastinal mets s/p XRT (2021) - in remission per chart review
# New melena with Hgb drop
NARRATIVE — tension → alternatives → commit → anticipate
Challenging case in an older patient with multiple comorbidities. [tension] Not a surgical candidate given the melena, and PCI of her distal LM would be prohibitively high-risk with ongoing GIB given the high-dose heparin required. [alternatives ruled out] Although the repeat echo has features of stress cardiomyopathy, the angiogram matches the rWMA territory (LAD), so this is CAD/ischemia until proven otherwise. [commit] She remains very high-risk for recurrent pulmonary edema (significant MR, ischemia ± ischemic MR), so we will watch BPs closely as we transition to a bumex gtt. [anticipate]
RECOMMENDATIONS — discrete orders, conditional logic, holds w/ triggers, cross-problem
1. NSTEMI
   a. Hold heparin gtt (see GIB)
   b. No Plavix at this time
   c. Can hold ASA if necessary from GI standpoint
   d. Atorvastatin 80 mg QD
2. HFrEF
   a. Bumex 1 mg IV → bumex gtt @ 0.5
   b. Nitro gtt — wean as able, goal SBP >100 and <130
   c. Hold ARB/SGLT2i/MRA now, layer on once more stable
   d. Trial metop IV 2.5 mg; if tolerates → tartrate 25 mg 1–2x/day
   e. BID lytes while diuresing, daily standing weights
3. GIB  [higher endoscopy risk 2/2 volume overload, hypoxia, CAD]
   a. Colonoscopy today
   b. High-dose PPI BID
   c. Hold heparin gtt
   d. Needs better access — PICC today
4. PNA
   a. CTX + doxycycline, 5–7 day CAP course
📁 CASE LIBRARY — full worked A&Ps for the bread-and-butter admits
▸

Tap a diagnosis → the spoken one-liner, the impression, the narrative paragraph, and the plan — written the way a seasoned resident would, with the connectors highlighted so you can see each one doing its job.

→ These 8 are a quick subset. The full case library — all systems, organized and searchable — lives in the
☑ THE SELF-CHECK BEFORE YOU SUBMIT
▸
☐ Does each problem line carry its key data inline?
☐ Did I stage/classify every diagnosis that has a system?
☐ Did I name my data gaps honestly?
☐ Is there a narrative paragraph with the central tension?
☐ Did I rule out the alternatives, not just state the plan?
☐ Did I commit to a working dx where there was uncertainty?
☐ Are my orders discrete, dosed, and routed — not vague sentences?
☐ Does every hold have a restart trigger?
☐ Did I give targets/goals, not just actions?
☐ Did I anticipate the next problem with a mechanism?
☐ Did I connect problems to each other where they interact?
— IF YOU'RE PRESENTING OUT LOUD —
☐ Could I say my one-liner in one breath, with the PMH curated to what matters today?
☐ Did I lead with my assessment, not a data dump?
☐ Two+ semantic qualifiers per active problem?
☐ Did I give pertinent negatives, not just positives?
☐ Calibrated confidence — no "maybe possibly," word matched to my certainty?
☐ Did I pre-load the "why not X" answer before they ask?
An intern says what. A fellow says what, why, why not the alternatives, and what's next — led by a crisp one-liner, in calibrated language, committing where it counts. Add the narrative, stage your diagnoses, dose your orders with logic, connect your problems, and say it out loud before rounds.
Case library
The bread-and-butter admits, written the way a seasoned resident would. Pick a diagnosis → the spoken one-liner, staged impression, narrative paragraph, and plan — with the connectors highlighted so you see each one doing its job. (The grammar behind them lives in the A&P tab.)
⌕
